    GitHub Issues¶

    ✅ #64: fix: flaky tests — test_hello_on_connect and scope backpressure¶

    Labels: bug

    Problem¶

    Known flaky tests: - TestControlChannel::test_hello_on_connect — asyncio TimeoutError (pre-existing, confirmed on clean main) - TestBackpressure::test_scope_* — _on_scope_frame attribute missing on ScopeHandler (tests for unimplemented functionality?)

    Solution¶

    • Fix or skip test_hello_on_connect (likely needs longer timeout or proper WebSocket handshake mock)
    • Scope backpressure: either implement _on_scope_frame or remove the dead tests

    Source: external code review

    ✅ #65: docs: generate API reference from docstrings¶

    Labels: documentation

    Problem¶

    MkDocs is configured (mkdocs.yml) but API reference pages are empty/missing. Docstrings exist on most public methods but aren't rendered.

    Solution¶

    • Add mkdocstrings[python] handler
    • Generate API docs for IcomRadio, StateCache, IcomCommander, CIV commands
    • Document BCD frequency limit (10 digits, max 9,999,999,999 Hz / ~10 GHz)

    Source: external code review

    ✅ #66: bug: CI-V GET commands timeout during scope streaming (release blocker)¶

    Labels: bug

    Problem¶

    When scope is active (~225 packets/sec on CI-V port 50002), GET commands (get_frequency 0x03, get_power 0x14, scope_enable 0x15) consistently timeout. SET commands (fire-and-forget) work fine.

    Audio (port 50003) and control (port 50001) are unaffected. Scope frames arrive fine through the same CI-V port. Only command ACK/RESPONSE frames are lost.

    Root Cause Analysis¶

    The CI-V RX loop architecture has a critical timing flaw:

    1. Single asyncio queue bottleneck: IcomTransport._packet_queue is an unbounded asyncio.Queue. All UDP packets (scope, ACK, responses) go into this single queue.

    2. Sequential processing: _civ_rx_loop() calls receive_packet(timeout=0.2) which does queue.get(). It processes ONE packet at a time, then iterates CI-V frames, then calls _route_civ_frame() which may await.

    3. The race: When a GET command is sent:

    4. _execute_civ_raw registers a Future via register_response(key)
    5. Sends the CI-V packet
    6. Awaits the Future with 2s timeout
    7. Meanwhile, _civ_rx_loop must dequeue the response packet, parse it, match it via resolve(), and set the Future result

    8. But scope floods the queue with ~225 packets/sec. The RX loop processes them one by one. By the time it reaches the ACK/response packet in the queue, 2 seconds have elapsed.

    9. Queue depth: At 225 scope packets/sec and 200ms timeout per receive_packet, the loop processes ~5 packets/iteration (burst). But each packet may contain multiple CI-V frames. The loop cannot keep up with scope flood AND process command responses in time.

    Evidence¶

    • Scope frames arrive fine (scope assembler works)
    • CI-V debug shows NO incoming non-scope frames at all during scope streaming
    • Audio on separate port works perfectly
    • Problem is identical on pre-#56 code (not a regression from fire-and-forget refactor)
    • wfview handles the same traffic fine (synchronous processing in single callback)

    Proposed Fix¶

    Priority processing for ACK/RESPONSE frames: Instead of a single FIFO queue, separate scope traffic from command responses at the transport or RX pump level.

    Options: A. Drain queue fully each iteration — process ALL pending packets in queue before sleeping, not just one B. Dual-queue in transport — separate scope packets from CI-V command packets at _handle_packet level C. Priority routing in RX pump — scan for ACK/RESPONSE frames first before processing scope chunks

    Option A is simplest and matches wfview's approach (process all pending datagrams in one callback).

    Release Blocker¶

    Without this fix, Web UI shows no frequency/mode/power on connect. State cache helps after first successful read, but initial read always fails when scope is active.

    ✅ #67: bug: civ_port=0 on reconnect — need retry in connect flow¶

    Labels: bug

    Problem¶

    After rapid disconnects/reconnects, the IC-7610 returns civ_port=0 and audio_port=0 in the status packet. Our fallback to default ports (50002/50003) sometimes works for scope but CI-V command responses may still fail.

    wfview handles this by retrying the connection — if status returns error (0xffffffff), it suggests rebooting the radio.

    Current behavior¶

    • Send conninfo with local ports → receive status with civ_port=0
    • Wait 5s, fallback to default ports
    • Sometimes works, sometimes doesn't

    Proposed fix¶

    Retry the status request: if civ_port=0, wait briefly and re-send conninfo up to 3 times before falling back to defaults. The radio often recovers within a few seconds.

    Files¶

    • src/icom_lan/_control_phase.py — _receive_civ_port()

    ✅ #68: Web UI v2.0: Wire mockup to live radio via WebSocket API¶

    Labels: enhancement

    Overview¶

    Replace the static/demo mockup (mockups/v2.html) with a fully functional Web UI connected to the icom-lan WebSocket API.

    The new UI (Tailwind CSS, glass-panel dark theme, IC-7610-inspired) is ready as a mockup with demo spectrum/waterfall animation. This issue tracks wiring it to the real radio.

    WebSocket Channels¶

    Channel Endpoint Format Purpose
    Control /api/v1/ws JSON Commands, state, events
    Scope /api/v1/scope Binary Spectrum + waterfall data
    Meters /api/v1/meters Binary S-meter, PWR, SWR, ALC, Id, TEMP
    Audio /api/v1/audio Binary RX/TX audio (Opus/PCM)

    Tasks¶

    Phase 1 — Core connectivity¶

    • [ ] Connect/Disconnect button → radio_connect / radio_disconnect
    • [ ] Connection state indicator (header dot + status bar)
    • [ ] Receive hello + state snapshot on connect
    • [ ] Display VFO A/B frequency from state
    • [ ] Display mode + filter from state

    Phase 2 — Live meters¶

    • [ ] Open /api/v1/meters WebSocket
    • [ ] Send meters_start with smeter/power/swr/alc
    • [ ] Parse binary meter frames (header + 3-byte records)
    • [ ] Update S-meter bars (green + red zones) for Main/Sub
    • [ ] Update center meters (ALC, SWR, PWR, Id, TEMP)
    • [ ] RX/TX badge switching based on PTT state

    Phase 3 — Controls → commands¶

    • [ ] Band buttons → set_freq command
    • [ ] Mode buttons → set_mode command
    • [ ] Filter buttons → set_filter command
    • [ ] ATT buttons → set_att command
    • [ ] PRE buttons → set_preamp command
    • [ ] AF Gain slider → set_af_level (new API)
    • [ ] RF Gain slider → set_rf_gain (new API)
    • [ ] TX Power slider → set_power command
    • [ ] PTT button → ptt command (press=on, release=off)

    Phase 4 — Live spectrum + waterfall¶

    • [ ] Open /api/v1/scope WebSocket
    • [ ] Parse binary scope frames (16-byte header + pixel array)
    • [ ] Render spectrum trace from pixel data (replacing demo animation)
    • [ ] Scroll waterfall from scope frames
    • [ ] Update frequency axis from scope start/end freq
    • [ ] Passband + center marker positioning

    Phase 5 — Audio¶

    • [ ] Open /api/v1/audio WebSocket
    • [ ] RX Audio button → audio_start / audio_stop
    • [ ] Decode audio frames (Opus/PCM) → Web Audio API playback
    • [ ] TX audio from browser microphone (existing functionality, new UI)
    • [ ] MONI button

    Phase 6 — Polish¶

    • [ ] Reconnect logic (auto-retry on WS drop)
    • [ ] Error handling + user-visible error messages
    • [ ] Replace mockups/v2.html → production static/index.html
    • [ ] Mobile/tablet responsive adjustments
    • [ ] Badge state sync (PRE/ATT/IP+/NB/NR) from radio events

    Design Reference¶

    Mockup: mockups/v2.html — Tailwind CSS, glass panels, cyan/amber VFOs, oscilloscope-grid spectrum, live demo waterfall.

    Dependencies¶

    • RF Gain / AF Level API already implemented (#68 scope)
    • Scope, Meters, Audio WebSocket handlers already exist
    • Control handler supports: set_freq, set_mode, set_filter, ptt, set_power, set_att, set_preamp, select_vfo, vfo_swap, vfo_equalize

    ✅ #69: Meters: single poller + broadcast instead of per-client CI-V polling¶

    Labels: bug, enhancement

    Problem¶

    Each meters WebSocket client spawns its own CI-V poll loop. With N clients, the CI-V bus gets N × fps read commands/sec. At 3 clients × 10fps = 30 reads/sec, the CI-V bus saturates and all commands (freq, mode, meters) start timing out.

    Solution¶

    Same pattern as scope: 1. Single meter poller in the server — polls CI-V at configured fps (e.g. 10fps) 2. Cached latest values — store last meter readings 3. Broadcast to all clients — each meters WS handler receives frames from the single poller 4. New clients get cached values immediately (no CI-V hit)

    Also needed¶

    • State cache for freq/mode/filter — state snapshot should read from cache, not CI-V
    • Connection state queries should be cached

    Impact¶

    • Fixes CI-V bus saturation with multiple browser tabs
    • Fixes meters/freq/mode timeouts under load
    • Reduces CI-V traffic from O(N×fps) to O(fps)

    ✅ #70: Audio: single RX broadcaster + shared stream instead of per-client start/stop¶

    Labels: bug, enhancement

    Problem¶

    Each AudioHandler calls start_audio_rx_opus() / stop_audio_rx_opus() independently. When a second browser tab connects, it stops the first tab's RX stream and starts its own. Result: only the last-connected client hears audio; all others go silent.

    This is the same pattern that #69 fixed for meters (per-client CI-V polling → bus saturation). Audio has the additional problem that stop is called before each start, actively breaking other clients.

    Solution¶

    Same broadcast pattern as scope and meters (after #69):

    RX Audio¶

    1. Single AudioBroadcaster in WebServer — calls start_audio_rx_opus() once
    2. Callback broadcasts encoded audio frames to all registered AudioHandler instances
    3. New clients register and immediately start receiving frames (no stream restart)
    4. Stream stops only when the last handler disconnects

    TX Audio¶

    • Only one client can TX at a time (radio has one TX path)
    • First client to send audio_start direction=tx gets TX lock
    • Others get an error response or queue
    • PTT release / disconnect releases the lock

    Impact¶

    • Fixes audio cutting out when multiple browser tabs are open
    • Prevents unnecessary stream stop/start cycles
    • Consistent architecture: scope, meters, and audio all use server-level broadcast

    Related¶

    • 69 — meters: single poller + broadcast (merged)¶

    • 68 — Web UI v2 wiring¶

    ✅ #72: refactor: RadioPoller — single CI-V serializer with command queue and state cache¶

    Summary¶

    Refactor the web server CI-V access pattern so that no client request ever touches the radio directly. All CI-V communication goes through a single RadioPoller task that serializes all reads and writes.

    Motivation¶

    Current architecture allows multiple concurrent CI-V accessors (ControlHandler state snapshot, MeterPoller, client commands) which can saturate the CI-V bus and cause timeouts. The radio has limited CI-V bandwidth and cannot handle concurrent requests reliably.

    Architecture¶

    RadioPoller — single CI-V accessor¶

    One asyncio.Task that is the only thing that ever sends CI-V commands:

    while True:
    # 1. Execute pending commands (if any)
    while not command_queue.empty():
        cmd = command_queue.get_nowait()
        await execute_command(cmd)    # fire-and-forget CI-V
        await asyncio.sleep(gap)      # inter-command gap

    # 2. Poll next parameter
    await poll_next()                 # get_freq / get_mode / get_smeter...
    await asyncio.sleep(gap)          # inter-poll gap
    • Polls all parameters at the same frequency (freq, mode, filter, ptt, smeter, power, swr, alc, temp, att, pre, etc.)
    • S-meter must be responsive — same poll rate as freq/mode
    • Commands (set_freq, set_mode, enable_scope, etc.) are inserted between poll steps via asyncio.Queue
    • Command deduplication: last-write-wins for freq/mode/filter/power; never dedup ptt_on/ptt_off
    • Absorbs current MeterPoller functionality (no separate meter polling task)

    CommandQueue¶

    • asyncio.Queue for write commands from clients
    • Commands: set_freq, set_mode, set_filter, set_power, set_att, set_pre, ptt_on/off, vfo_swap, set_rf_gain, set_af_level, enable_scope, disable_scope
    • All fire-and-forget (no blocking CI-V reads from client path)

    RadioStateCache (expanded dataclass)¶

    • Extend existing state_cache to include ALL polled parameters
    • Design as an extensible @dataclass — new parameters = add a field
    • Architecture must anticipate: different radios with different parameters (IC-7610, IC-7300, FTX-1, etc.)
    • Consider a base RadioStateCache with rig-specific subclasses
    • Fields: freq_a, freq_b (if available), mode, filter, ptt, smeter, power_out, swr, alc, id_current, temp, att, pre, rf_gain, af_level, nb, nr, etc.
    • Each field has a timestamp for staleness detection

    State broadcast¶

    • When RadioPoller updates the cache and detects a change, it calls server.broadcast_event(name, data) which pushes to all connected ControlHandler WebSockets
    • Events: freq_changed, mode_changed, ptt, meters_updated, etc.
    • ControlHandler _send_state_snapshot() reads cache only (zero CI-V, instant)
    • Current state_poll_loop in ControlHandler is removed (RadioPoller replaces it)

    Client-side (Web UI)¶

    • Client has its own poll loop that periodically syncs state from server cache (e.g., re-reads state every ~300ms or on WS reconnect)
    • Server push events are supplementary — they make updates faster, but the poll loop provides consistency if an event is missed
    • Existing state_poll_loop per-ControlHandler → replaced by RadioPoller broadcast

    Scope and Audio¶

    • Scope/audio data streams remain unchanged (separate WS, binary broadcast)
    • enable_scope / disable_scope go through CommandQueue
    • Audio enable/disable through CommandQueue

    Constraints¶

    • No dead code. Remove old code paths that are replaced. Do not leave commented-out blocks or unused functions.
    • No code duplication. Extend/modify existing code rather than writing parallel implementations.
    • Maximize reuse of existing state_cache, MeterPoller logic, handler infrastructure.
    • Python 3.11+, no new dependencies
    • All existing tests must pass (except pre-existing TestControlChannel flakes)
    • Do not break binary meter frame protocol (protocol.py encode_meter_frame), audio, or scope handlers

    Files likely affected¶

    • src/icom_lan/web/server.py — RadioPoller, CommandQueue, expanded cache, broadcast
    • src/icom_lan/web/handlers.py — ControlHandler becomes thin (cache reads + queue writes), MetersHandler simplified
    • src/icom_lan/radio.py or src/icom_lan/state.py — expanded RadioStateCache dataclass
    • src/icom_lan/web/static/index.html — client poll loop + event handling
    • tests/test_web_server.py — updated tests

    References¶

    • references/wfview/ — rig definitions for future rig-description-based architecture
    • Current MeterPoller in server.py — absorb into RadioPoller
    • Current state_cache in radio object — extend, do not replace

    Closes follow-up to #69, #68.

    ✅ #73: feat: UDP relay proxy for remote access via VPN (Tailscale)¶

    Summary¶

    Transparent UDP relay that proxies Icom LAN protocol traffic, enabling remote radio access through VPN (e.g., Tailscale).

    Use case¶

    Radio (IC-7610) is on local LAN. User connects via Tailscale VPN from phone/laptop and uses wfview, RB1, or any compatible client — pointing at the proxy IP instead of the radio IP.

    Architecture¶

    Simple transparent UDP relay — no protocol parsing, just packet forwarding:

    Client (wfview via VPN) → Proxy (:50001/:50002/:50003) → Radio (192.168.55.40)
Client ← Proxy ← Radio

    Implementation¶

    • New CLI subcommand: icom-lan proxy --radio 192.168.55.40 --listen 0.0.0.0
    • Listens on 3 UDP ports (50001, 50002, 50003)
    • For each port: receives packet from client, forwards to radio; receives from radio, forwards back to client
    • Session tracking by source address (map client addr → radio conversation)
    • Timeout inactive sessions after ~60s
    • ~50-100 lines of asyncio UDP code

    Constraints¶

    • No protocol parsing (transparent relay)
    • Client must manually enter proxy IP in their app (no broadcast discovery forwarding)
    • One client per proxy instance (one LAN slot consumed on radio)
    • Python 3.11+, no new dependencies

    Future (out of scope)¶

    • Protocol-aware proxy with discovery interception
    • Multi-client multiplexing (share one LAN slot)
    • Integration with web server (proxy + web UI on same slot)

    ✅ #74: Ironclad backend: server owns radio connection exclusively¶

    Problem¶

    The web server radio connection is fragile: 1. kill without graceful shutdown leaves "ghost" LAN slots occupied for 30-90s 2. IC-7610 has only 2-3 LAN slots; ungraceful kills quickly exhaust them 3. Frontend WS disconnects can trigger radio disconnect (soft_disconnect in handlers) 4. Scope enable/disable tied to frontend WS lifecycle 5. get_frequency() uses 10s cache TTL + dedupe, masking real frequency changes from RadioPoller 6. No auto-reconnect when radio connection drops

    Principle¶

    Backend = sole owner of the radio. Frontend is a viewer + command sender.

    No external event (browser tab close, WS disconnect, network glitch) should ever kill or degrade the radio connection.

    Solution¶

    Phase 1: Connection resilience (immediate)¶

    • [x] Graceful shutdown: SIGTERM/SIGINT → soft_disconnect() → slots freed immediately
    • [x] RadioPoller: all CI-V through single task with 2s timeouts
    • [x] Scope enable/disable through CommandQueue (no direct radio calls)
    • [ ] Remove soft_disconnect() from ControlHandler disconnect path — radio lives while server lives
    • [ ] Cache bypass for RadioPoller polls (invalidate before each get_* call, or bypass dedupe)
    • [ ] Reduce or remove _cache_ttl_freq / _cache_ttl_mode for poller context

    Phase 2: Always-on architecture¶

    • [ ] Scope managed by server, not by WS client lifecycle (always enabled, or server-policy)
    • [ ] Auto-reconnect: if radio disconnects, server retries with exponential backoff
    • [ ] Slot tracking: know how many slots are in use, refuse new connections if full
    • [ ] Health endpoint: /api/v1/health with radio connection status, slot count, poller stats

    Phase 3: Multi-radio readiness¶

    • [ ] Base RadioStateCache with rig-specific subclasses
    • [ ] Rig description files (YAML) for parameter sets per model
    • [ ] RadioPoller parameterized by rig description

    Context¶

    • IC-7610: 2-3 LAN connection slots, ~30-90s ghost slot timeout
    • CI-V + scope share UDP port 50002 — scope frames can interfere with CI-V responses
    • _DEFAULT_CACHE_TTL = {"freq": 10.0, "mode": 10.0} in radio.py:127
    • dedupe=True on get_frequency() / get_power() / get_rf_gain() / get_af_level()
    • Scope data = CI-V frames on port 50002, mixed with query responses

    Relates to #72 (RadioPoller).

    ✅ #75: feat: CI-V Auto Information mode — radio pushes meters instead of polling¶

    Problem¶

    S-meter update rate is unacceptably slow (~1.5s cycle) because RadioPoller manually polls every meter via CI-V GET commands (0x15 0x02, 0x15 0x11, etc.). Each poll adds load to the CI-V bus and competes with scope frames on port 50002.

    Aggressive polling (50ms intervals) causes CI-V bus saturation and connection freezes.

    How wfview solves this¶

    wfview enables CI-V Auto Information mode at connect time (wfmain.cpp:3734):

    queue->add(priorityImmediate, queueItem(funcAutoInformation, QVariant::fromValue(uchar(2)), false, 0));
// Then enables individual meters in the auto-information stream:
queue->add(priorityImmediate, queueItem(funcALCMeter, QVariant::fromValue(uchar(1)), false, 0));
queue->add(priorityImmediate, queueItem(funcCompMeter, QVariant::fromValue(uchar(1)), false, 0));
queue->add(priorityImmediate, queueItem(funcIdMeter, QVariant::fromValue(uchar(1)), false, 0));
queue->add(priorityImmediate, queueItem(funcVdMeter, QVariant::fromValue(uchar(1)), false, 0));
queue->add(priorityImmediate, queueItem(funcSWRMeter, QVariant::fromValue(uchar(1)), false, 0));

    After this, the IC-7610 pushes meter updates (S-meter, power, SWR, ALC, etc.) as unsolicited CI-V frames — no polling needed. Freq and mode changes also come unsolicited via CI-V transceive.

    Key details from IC-7610.rig¶

    • S Meter supports Command29=true (extended CI-V protocol)
    • S Meter is in Periodic list with Priority=Highest
    • IC-7610 has HasFDComms=true → queue interval = 25ms (vs 100ms for serial)

    Solution¶

    Phase 1: Enable CI-V transceive / auto-information at connect¶

    1. Send CI-V auto-information enable command at connect time (after open_close)
    2. Enable individual meter streams (ALC, SWR, power, etc.)
    3. Our _update_state_cache_from_frame() in _civ_rx.py already parses all unsolicited frames — no changes needed there

    Phase 2: Remove meter polling from RadioPoller¶

    1. Remove S-meter, power, SWR, ALC from _QUERY_CMDS
    2. Keep only params not covered by auto-information (RF gain, AF level, attenuator, preamp)
    3. Increase _QUERY_INTERVAL for remaining slow params (they rarely change)

    Phase 3: Frontend smoothing¶

    1. CSS transition-all duration-150 on meter bars is already present
    2. Consider requestAnimationFrame batching if updates come faster than 60fps

    Expected result¶

    • S-meter updates at radio-native rate (~20-30 fps) instead of ~0.7 fps
    • Zero CI-V polling overhead for meters
    • Reduced CI-V bus contention → fewer freezes
    • Same architecture as wfview

    References¶

    • references/wfview/src/wfmain.cpp:3734 — auto-information setup
    • references/wfview/rigs/IC-7610.rig — Command29, periodic priorities
    • IC-7610 CI-V Programming Manual — command 0x1A 0x05 format

    Related¶

    • 72 — RadioPoller architecture¶

    • 74 — Ironclad backend (CI-V bus contention is a root cause of freezes)¶

    ✅ #76: UI audit: systematic review and fix of all web interface issues¶

    Labels: ui

    Problem¶

    Web UI has accumulated bugs through rapid development. Need a systematic audit and fix cycle instead of ad-hoc hotfixes.

    Audit checklist¶

    Meters¶

    • [x] S-meter: correct scale (-54 to +60 dB), updates at ~10fps
    • [x] PWR meter: shows set watts during RX, measured during TX
    • [x] Id meter: PA drain current (0A idle is correct)
    • [x] Vd meter: voltage (13.8V nominal)
    • [x] Click on PWR bar sets output power

    VFO / Frequency¶

    • [x] Click on waterfall sets frequency (snap to tuning step)
    • [x] Wheel tuning works (scroll on waterfall)
    • [x] Band buttons set correct center frequencies

    Mode / Filter¶

    • [x] Mode buttons (USB/LSB/CW/CWR/AM/FM/RTTY) work
    • [x] Filter buttons (FIL1/FIL2/FIL3) work
    • [x] Mode/filter display updates from radio changes
    • [x] Passband overlay on waterfall matches mode

    Controls¶

    • [x] ATT buttons (OFF/6/12/18) work
    • [x] RF gain slider works
    • [x] SQL slider works (if connected)
    • [x] TX Power slider works

    Scope / Waterfall¶

    • [x] Spectrum displays correctly
    • [x] Waterfall scrolls
    • [x] Marker at center frequency

    Audio¶

    • [x] RX audio toggle works
    • [x] Audio plays without delay
    • [x] Audio stops immediately on toggle off
    • [x] Audio flush on frequency change
    • [x] No audio dropout during normal operation

    Connection¶

    • [ ] Auto-reconnect after CI-V dropout
    • [x] Page refresh recovers cleanly

    Stale/suspect elements¶

    Process¶

    For each bug found: 1. Describe in comment or checkbox 2. Fix with commit referencing this issue 3. Verify on live radio before checking off 4. Do not check off untested items

    Baseline¶

    • Tag: dev-stable / v0.8.3
    • Branch: refactor/radio-poller

    ✅ #77: VFO A/B frequency display updates on knob turn¶

    Labels: type:feature, type:tech-debt, ui

    ✅ #78: VFO A/B select buttons work¶

    ✅ #79: VFO swap button works¶

    ✅ #80: Preamp buttons (OFF/1/2) work¶

    ✅ #81: AF level slider works¶

    ✅ #82: Frequency axis labels correct¶

    ✅ #83: Status indicators (connected/disconnected)¶

    ✅ #84: PTT button works (on/off)¶

    ✅ #85: tempStatus DOM id — TEMP meter removed, cleanup needed?¶

    ✅ #86: moniBtn — what does it do? Connected?¶

    ✅ #87: sqlSlider / sqlVal — squelch implemented?¶

    ✅ #88: txPwrSlider / txPwrVal — duplicate of PWR bar click?¶

    ✅ #89: SWR meter: calibrated values, color coding¶

    ✅ #90: ALC meter: OVER indicator¶

    ✅ #91: Dual-receiver state model + HTTP poll endpoint¶

    Labels: ui

    Dual-Receiver State Model + HTTP Poll Endpoint¶

    Replace per-event WS state notifications with a unified RadioState model served via GET /api/v1/state (polled every 200ms by frontend). Dual-receiver (MAIN/SUB) support via CI-V Command29 prefix.

    Research Summary¶

    CI-V Command29¶

    IC-7610 HasCommand29=true. Prefix 0x29 0x00 (MAIN) or 0x29 0x01 (SUB) before any cmd29-capable command to address a specific receiver without switching active VFO.

    Per-receiver commands (cmd29=true): ATT (0x11), AF Gain (0x14/01), RF Gain (0x14/02), Squelch (0x14/03), NB Level (0x14/12), Preamp (0x16/02), NB (0x16/22), NR (0x16/40), Auto Notch (0x16/41), Manual Notch (0x16/48), DIGI-SEL (0x16/4E), Filter Shape (0x16/56), IP+ (0x16/65), Filter Width (0x1A/03), S Meter (0x15/02)

    Built-in receiver byte (no cmd29 needed): RX Frequency (0x25), RX Mode (0x26) — payload includes 00 (MAIN) or 01 (SUB)

    Global (not per-receiver): PTT (0x1C/00), RF Power (0x14/0A), Split (0x0F), TX meters (0x15/11-16)

    CI-V Transceive Mode¶

    IC-7610 pushes only cmd 0x00 (freq) and 0x01 (mode) on front-panel changes. No push for NB/NR/ATT/preamp/etc. No "Auto Information" support (that is IC-705/IC-7851+).

    Polling Budget¶

    Interleaved meter/state at 25ms intervals (M-S-M-S pattern): - Meters: 20 Hz effective (S-meter, Power, SWR, ALC, Vd, Id round-robin) - State: 20 Hz effective — 25 params × 50ms = 1.25s full cycle for both receivers - Band-change burst: 12 critical params × 25ms = 300ms - Compare: wfview MediumLow tier = 5-6s for same params

    Update Latency¶

    Source Latency Method
    Web UI click 0ms Optimistic update + poll-back confirm
    Front panel change (freq/mode) 0ms CI-V Transceive push
    Front panel change (NB/NR/ATT/etc) ≤1.25s Background sweep
    Band change 300ms Burst poll

    Data Model¶

    src/icom_lan/radio_state.py (new file)¶

    from __future__ import annotations
from dataclasses import dataclass, field, asdict

@dataclass(slots=True)
class ReceiverState:
    """Per-receiver (MAIN or SUB) state."""
    freq: int = 0
    mode: str = "USB"
    filter: int | None = None
    data_mode: bool = False
    att: int = 0           # dB: 0, 3, 6, ..., 45
    preamp: int = 0        # 0=off, 1=P1, 2=P2
    nb: bool = False
    nr: bool = False
    digisel: bool = False
    ipplus: bool = False
    af_level: int = 0      # 0-255
    rf_gain: int = 0       # 0-255
    squelch: int = 0       # 0-255
    s_meter: int = 0       # raw 0-241

@dataclass(slots=True)
class RadioState:
    """Full radio state: two receivers + global params."""
    main: ReceiverState = field(default_factory=ReceiverState)
    sub: ReceiverState = field(default_factory=ReceiverState)
    active: str = "MAIN"    # "MAIN" | "SUB"
    ptt: bool = False
    power_level: int = 0    # TX power 0-255
    split: bool = False

    def to_dict(self) -> dict:
        return {
            "active": self.active,
            "ptt": self.ptt,
            "power_level": self.power_level,
            "split": self.split,
            "main": asdict(self.main),
            "sub": asdict(self.sub),
        }

    def receiver(self, which: str) -> ReceiverState:
        """Get receiver by name: \"MAIN\" or \"SUB\"."""
        return self.main if which == "MAIN" else self.sub

    Polling Architecture¶

    State Poller (new class StatePoller)¶

    Replaces state-related parts of RadioPoller. Runs as a separate asyncio task.

    ┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐
│              25ms tick                   │
│  ┌─────┐  ┌─────┐  ┌─────┐  ┌─────┐    │
│  │Meter│  │State│  │Meter│  │State│ …  │
│  │query│  │query│  │query│  │query│    │
│  └─────┘  └─────┘  └─────┘  └─────┘    │
│     ↓        ↓        ↓        ↓        │
│  S-meter  ATT(M)   Power   ATT(S)  …   │
└─────────────────────────────────────────┘

    Meter round-robin (slot 0, 2, 4, ...): [S-meter, Power, SWR, ALC, Vd, Id] — 6 items, 150ms cycle

    State round-robin (slot 1, 3, 5, ...): 

    _STATE_QUERIES = [
    # Per-receiver (cmd29 prefix), polled for MAIN then SUB
    (0x11, None, True),       # ATT
    (0x14, 0x01, True),       # AF gain
    (0x14, 0x02, True),       # RF gain
    (0x14, 0x03, True),       # Squelch
    (0x16, 0x02, True),       # Preamp
    (0x16, 0x22, True),       # NB
    (0x16, 0x40, True),       # NR
    (0x16, 0x4E, True),       # DIGI-SEL
    (0x16, 0x65, True),       # IP+
    (0x1A, 0x03, True),       # Filter width
    (0x1A, 0x06, True),       # Data mode
    # Global (no cmd29)
    (0x1C, 0x00, False),      # PTT
    (0x14, 0x0A, False),      # Power level
    (0x0F, None, False),      # Split
]
# Per-rx: 11 × 2 receivers = 22 queries
# Global: 3 queries
# Total: 25 queries × 50ms = 1.25s cycle

    Interleave pattern: state_queries alternate MAIN/SUB: ATT(M), ATT(S), AF(M), AF(S), ... PTT, PowerLvl, Split

    Band-change burst: On freq delta > 500kHz, inject high-priority burst of all per-rx params for that receiver.

    Command29 Implementation¶

    New method in IcomTransport or commands.py:

    def build_civ_frame(cmd, sub=None, data=b"", receiver=None):
    """Build CI-V frame with optional cmd29 prefix."""
    payload = bytearray()
    if receiver is not None:
        payload.append(0x29)
        payload.append(receiver)  # 0x00=MAIN, 0x01=SUB
    payload.append(cmd)
    if sub is not None:
        payload.append(sub)
    payload.extend(data)
    return bytes(payload)

    Response Parsing¶

    In _civ_rx.py, detect cmd29 prefix in responses:

    if frame.cmd == 0x29:
    receiver = frame.data[0]  # 0x00=MAIN, 0x01=SUB
    real_cmd = frame.data[1]
    real_data = frame.data[2:]
    # Route to correct ReceiverState

    For 0x25/0x26 responses: 

    if frame.cmd == 0x25:
    # Last 2 bytes = receiver (00=MAIN, 01=SUB)
    receiver = frame.data[-1]
    freq_bcd = frame.data[:-1]

    HTTP Endpoint¶

    GET /api/v1/state¶

    @routes.get("/api/v1/state")
async def get_state(request):
    state = request.app["radio_state"]
    return web.json_response(state.to_dict())

    Response (~500 bytes): 

    {
  "active": "MAIN",
  "ptt": false,
  "power_level": 100,
  "split": false,
  "main": {
    "freq": 14074000, "mode": "USB", "filter": 1,
    "data_mode": false, "att": 0, "preamp": 1,
    "nb": true, "nr": false, "digisel": true, "ipplus": false,
    "af_level": 200, "rf_gain": 255, "squelch": 0, "s_meter": 37
  },
  "sub": {
    "freq": 7074000, "mode": "LSB", "filter": 2,
    "data_mode": false, "att": 0, "preamp": 0,
    "nb": false, "nr": false, "digisel": false, "ipplus": false,
    "af_level": 180, "rf_gain": 255, "squelch": 0, "s_meter": 12
  }
}

    Frontend¶

    Poll loop¶

    let prevState = null;
setInterval(async () => {
    const r = await fetch("/api/v1/state");
    const s = await r.json();
    if (!prevState) applyFullState(s);
    else applyDiff(prevState, s);
    prevState = s;
}, 200);

    Diff engine¶

    function applyDiff(prev, next) {
    // Global
    if (prev.ptt !== next.ptt) updatePtt(next.ptt);
    if (prev.active !== next.active) updateActiveReceiver(next.active);
    if (prev.split !== next.split) updateSplit(next.split);
    if (prev.power_level !== next.power_level) updatePowerLevel(next.power_level);
    // Per-receiver
    diffReceiver("main", prev.main, next.main);
    diffReceiver("sub", prev.sub, next.sub);
}

function diffReceiver(rx, prev, next) {
    for (const key of Object.keys(next)) {
        if (prev[key] !== next[key]) {
            updateField(rx, key, next[key]);
        }
    }
}

    WebSocket keeps¶

    • Binary meters (40fps) — scope, waterfall
    • Audio RX/TX
    • Command responses + error toasts

    Implementation Phases¶

    Phase 1: Backend model + endpoint (no frontend breakage)¶

    Files to create/modify: - [ ] NEW src/icom_lan/radio_state.py — ReceiverState, RadioState dataclasses - [ ] MOD src/icom_lan/commands.py — build_query(cmd, sub, receiver) with cmd29 prefix - [ ] MOD src/icom_lan/_civ_rx.py: - Parse cmd29 prefix in responses → route to RadioState.main or .sub - Parse 0x25 response (freq + receiver byte) - Parse 0x26 response (mode + receiver byte) - Handle unsolicited 0x00/0x01 (Transceive mode) - Update RadioState fields alongside existing StateCache - [ ] MOD src/icom_lan/web/radio_poller.py: - Add state queries with cmd29 prefix - Interleave meter/state in 25ms cycle - Band-change burst detection - [ ] MOD src/icom_lan/web/server.py: - Create RadioState instance - Add GET /api/v1/state route - Enable CI-V Transceive mode on connect (1A 05 0112 01) - [ ] NEW tests/test_radio_state.py — unit tests for RadioState - [ ] NEW tests/test_cmd29.py — cmd29 frame building/parsing tests

    Verification: curl localhost:8080/api/v1/state returns valid JSON with both receivers.

    Phase 2: Frontend switch to HTTP poll¶

    • [ ] MOD index.html:
    • Add setInterval(fetch, 200) + diff engine
    • Remove WS event handlers for state (freq_changed, mode_changed, ptt, nb_changed, etc.)
    • Dual-receiver display (MAIN/SUB)
    • Keep WS for meters/scope/audio

    Phase 3: Cleanup¶

    • [ ] Remove WS state event broadcasting from server
    • [ ] Remove _notify_change() calls for state in _civ_rx.py
    • [ ] Migrate or deprecate old StateCache (keep for rigctld compatibility)
    • [ ] Update all tests
    • [ ] Tag dev-stable

    ✅ #92: Dual-receiver support: cmd29 routing, SUB scope, bidirectional sync¶

    Labels: enhancement

    Summary¶

    IC-7610 is a dual-receiver radio — MAIN and SUB work simultaneously. The current web UI treats them as two displays but all control commands (tune, mode, filter, AF/RF gain, etc.) always target MAIN. Switching to SUB VFO only changes the UI visuals ("картинка") — nothing real happens.

    Root Cause¶

    All SET commands (0x05 set_freq, 0x06 set_mode, etc.) go out without receiver addressing. IC-7610 has hasCommand29=true, meaning every per-receiver command must be wrapped in 0x29 <receiver> <cmd> to target the correct receiver. Without cmd29, commands implicitly go to the radio's currently-selected VFO — which may or may not match what the web UI thinks is active.

    How wfview Does It Right (reference: references/wfview/)¶

    1. currentReceiver is the single source of truth — every command carries a receiver byte. When it changes, all subsequent commands automatically target the new receiver.

    2. cmd29 wrapping for IC-7610 (icomcommander.cpp:287-292): 

      if (rigCaps.hasCommand29 && it.value().cmd29) {
    payload.append(0x29);
    payload.append(static_cast<uchar>(receiver));
}

    3. Reactive sync via polling — wfview polls 0x07 0xD2 (funcVFOBandMS) periodically to detect physical VFO switch on the radio. Software follows the radio, not just its own commands.

    4. Scope follows receiver (wfmain.cpp:5663-5666): 

      if (currentReceiver != r) {
    if (!rigCaps->hasCommand29 && rigCaps->commands.contains(funcScopeMainSub))
        queue->add(priorityImmediate, queueItem(funcScopeMainSub, val.value, false, 0));
    currentReceiver = r;
}

    5. Dual Watch polled — 0x07 0xC2 queried periodically (IC-7610.rig: Periodic\32\Command=VFO Dual Watch).

    CI-V Command Reference (IC-7610)¶

    VFO commands (0x07)¶

    Bytes Function Notes
    0x07 0xB0 VFO Swap M/S Swap MAIN↔SUB
    0x07 0xC0 Dual Watch Off
    0x07 0xC1 Dual Watch On
    0x07 0xC2 Dual Watch Query Returns 0x00/0x01
    0x07 0xD0 Select MAIN Makes MAIN the active receiver
    0x07 0xD1 Select SUB Makes SUB the active receiver
    0x07 0xD2 Active Receiver Query Returns 0x00=MAIN, 0x01=SUB

    cmd29 wrapping¶

    Without cmd29:  FE FE <to> <from> 05 <bcd_freq> FD        → targets "active" VFO
With cmd29:     FE FE <to> <from> 29 <rx> 05 <bcd_freq> FD  → targets specific receiver
    Where <rx>: 0x00=MAIN, 0x01=SUB.

    Scope commands (0x27)¶

    Sub Function Values
    0x10 Scope on/off 0x00=off, 0x01=on
    0x11 Scope data output 0x00=off, 0x01=on
    0x12 Scope MAIN/SUB select 0x00=MAIN, 0x01=SUB
    0x13 Scope single/dual mode 0x00=single, 0x01=dual

    SUB scope requires Dual Watch enabled on the radio. CI-V cannot enable it programmatically.

    Commands that need cmd29 (per-receiver)¶

    • 0x05 — Set frequency
    • 0x06 — Set mode
    • 0x07 — VFO mode select
    • 0x11 — Attenuator
    • 0x14 0x01 — AF level
    • 0x14 0x02 — RF gain
    • 0x14 0x03 — Squelch
    • 0x16 0x02 — Preamp
    • 0x16 0x22 — NB
    • 0x16 0x40 — NR
    • 0x16 0x4E — DIGI-SEL
    • 0x16 0x65 — IP+

    Commands that are global (no cmd29)¶

    • 0x1C 0x00 — PTT
    • 0x14 0x0A — TX power
    • 0x0F — Split

    Architecture (current state)¶

    What already works¶

    • RadioState with main: ReceiverState + sub: ReceiverState + active: str (radio_state.py)
    • ScopeAssembler with independent MAIN/SUB assembly (scope.py)
    • Dual-receiver polling in _STATE_QUERIES via cmd29 for GET (radio_poller.py)
    • _civ_rx.py parses cmd29-wrapped responses, routes to correct receiver
    • commands.py has scope_main_sub(), scope_single_dual()
    • Frontend HTTP poll reads both receivers from /api/v1/state

    What is broken¶

    1. All SET commands go without cmd29 — always target implicit "active" VFO
    2. Frontend sends commands without receiver parameter — always controls MAIN
    3. No polling of 0x07 0xD2 — can't detect physical VFO switch on radio
    4. No polling of 0x07 0xC2 — can't know Dual Watch state
    5. Scope hardcoded to MAIN — renderLoop filters frame.receiver !== 0
    6. Audio — unclear if LAN audio follows 0x07 0xD0/0xD1 switch (needs testing)

    Implementation Phases¶

    Phase 1: cmd29 command routing (backend) → #93¶

    • [ ] Add receiver field to SET command dataclasses
    • [ ] Implement cmd29 wrapping in _execute()
    • [ ] Update handlers.py to pass receiver from frontend params

    Phase 2: Receiver-aware frontend → #94¶

    • [ ] All sendCommand calls include receiver
    • [ ] Controls show active receiver values on VFO switch
    • [ ] Tuning targets active receiver

    Phase 3: SUB scope support → #95¶

    • [ ] Scope follows active receiver (partially done on branch)
    • [ ] Scope health monitor awareness
    • [ ] Integrate with Dual Watch state from #96

    Phase 4: Bidirectional sync & Dual Watch → #96¶

    • [ ] Poll 0x07 0xD2 (active receiver query)
    • [ ] Poll 0x07 0xC2 (Dual Watch state)
    • [ ] Frontend reacts to physical VFO switch
    • [ ] DW indicator in UI

    Investigation: LAN audio routing → #97¶

    • [ ] Test if audio follows VFO switch

    See sub-issues for details.

    Key Files¶

    File Role
    src/icom_lan/web/radio_poller.py CI-V command serializer, SET commands, polling
    src/icom_lan/web/handlers.py WebSocket command dispatch (ControlHandler)
    src/icom_lan/web/static/index.html Frontend (single-file app)
    src/icom_lan/web/server.py WebServer, scope broadcast, meter broadcast
    src/icom_lan/radio_state.py RadioState model (MAIN + SUB + active)
    src/icom_lan/_civ_rx.py CI-V RX parsing, state cache updates
    src/icom_lan/commands.py CI-V frame builders
    src/icom_lan/scope.py ScopeAssembler (dual receiver)
    references/wfview/ wfview source (reference implementation)

    wfview Reference Paths¶

    What File:Line
    cmd29 wrapping src/radio/icomcommander.cpp:287-294
    funcVFOBandMS handler src/wfmain.cpp:5656-5676
    Scope follows receiver src/wfmain.cpp:5663-5666
    getVfoCommand (cmd routing) src/cachingqueue.cpp:215-260
    IC-7610 rig definition rigs/IC-7610.rig
    Periodic polls (including DW, scope M/S) rigs/IC-7610.rig (Periodic section)

    ✅ #93: Phase 1: cmd29 command routing for dual-receiver SET operations¶

    Labels: enhancement

    Parent: #92¶

    Goal¶

    All per-receiver SET commands must be wrapped in cmd29 (0x29 <receiver> <cmd> [sub] [data]) so they target the correct receiver (MAIN or SUB) instead of implicitly going to whatever VFO the radio currently has selected.

    What to change¶

    1. Add receiver field to all per-receiver command dataclasses¶

    File: src/icom_lan/web/radio_poller.py

    @dataclass(frozen=True, slots=True)
class SetFreq:
    freq: int
    receiver: int = 0  # 0=MAIN, 1=SUB

@dataclass(frozen=True, slots=True)
class SetMode:
    mode: str
    filter_width: int | None = None
    receiver: int = 0

# Same for: SetFilter, SetAfLevel, SetRfGain, SetSquelch, SetNB, SetNR, 
# SetDigiSel, SetIpPlus, SetAttenuator, SetPreamp

    Global commands (NO receiver): PttOn, PttOff, SetPower, SetBand, VfoSwap, VfoEqualize, EnableScope, DisableScope.

    2. Implement cmd29 wrapping in _execute()¶

    File: src/icom_lan/web/radio_poller.py

    Option A — helper method: 

    async def _send_cmd29(self, receiver: int, cmd: int, sub: int | None = None, data: bytes = b"") -> None:
    """Send CI-V command wrapped in cmd29 for specific receiver."""
    inner = bytes([receiver, cmd])
    if sub is not None:
        inner += bytes([sub])
    inner += data
    await self._radio.send_civ(0x29, data=inner, wait_response=False)

    Option B — extend radio.send_civ() with optional receiver parameter.

    Then in _execute(): 

    case SetFreq(freq=freq, receiver=rx):
    from ..commands import bcd_encode  # or equivalent
    await self._send_cmd29(rx, 0x05, data=bcd_encode_freq(freq))

case SetMode(mode=mode, receiver=rx):
    mode_byte = MODE_MAP[mode]
    await self._send_cmd29(rx, 0x06, data=bytes([mode_byte]))

case SetAfLevel(level=level, receiver=rx):
    await self._send_cmd29(rx, 0x14, sub=0x01, data=encode_level(level))

# etc.

    3. Update handlers.py to pass receiver¶

    File: src/icom_lan/web/handlers.py

    _enqueue_command() must extract receiver from params: 

    case "set_freq":
    freq = int(params["freq"])
    rx = int(params.get("receiver", 0))
    q.put(SetFreq(freq, receiver=rx))
    return {"freq": freq, "receiver": rx}

    Commands → cmd29 mapping¶

    Command CI-V Sub cmd29
    SetFreq 0x05 — ✅
    SetMode 0x06 — ✅
    SetFilter 0x06 — ✅ (part of mode)
    SetAttenuator 0x11 — ✅
    SetAfLevel 0x14 0x01 ✅
    SetRfGain 0x14 0x02 ✅
    SetSquelch 0x14 0x03 ✅
    SetPreamp 0x16 0x02 ✅
    SetNB 0x16 0x22 ✅
    SetNR 0x16 0x40 ✅
    SetDigiSel 0x16 0x4E ✅
    SetIpPlus 0x16 0x65 ✅
    PttOn/Off 0x1C 0x00 ❌ global
    SetPower 0x14 0x0A ❌ global

    4. Verify BCD encoding¶

    radio.set_frequency() currently handles BCD encoding internally. When sending via cmd29, we need access to the raw BCD encoder. Check if commands.py exports a BCD freq encoder, or extract from set_frequency().

    5. Tests¶

    • Test cmd29 frame construction for each command type
    • Test that receiver=0 and receiver=1 produce different cmd29 payloads
    • Test that global commands (PTT, power) are NOT wrapped in cmd29
    • Run full test suite to verify no regressions

    Important notes¶

    • Fire-and-forget only — all sends must use wait_response=False (see radio_poller.py module docstring)
    • The existing _STATE_QUERIES already use cmd29 for GET — this phase adds cmd29 for SET
    • _civ_rx.py already parses cmd29-wrapped responses — no changes needed there
    • Default receiver=0 preserves backward compatibility

    ✅ #94: Phase 2: Receiver-aware frontend (route controls to active receiver)¶

    Labels: enhancement, ui

    Parent: #92¶

    Depends on: #93 (cmd29 routing)¶

    Goal¶

    When the user switches to SUB VFO in the web UI, all controls (tuning, mode, filter, AF/RF gain, ATT, PRE, NB, NR, SQL) must target the SUB receiver. Currently they always target MAIN regardless of which VFO is selected.

    What to change¶

    1. All sendCommand calls must include receiver¶

    File: src/icom_lan/web/static/index.html

    Every sendCommand() call that targets a specific receiver must pass the active receiver:

    // Current:
sendCommand('set_freq', { freq: f });

// Fixed:
var rx = state.activeVfo === 'B' ? 1 : 0;
sendCommand('set_freq', { freq: f, receiver: rx });

    Affected commands: - set_freq (tuning via wheel, waterfall click, direct input) - set_mode (mode buttons) - set_filter (filter buttons FIL1/FIL2/FIL3) - set_att (attenuator buttons) - set_preamp (preamp buttons) - set_af_level (AF gain slider) - set_rf_gain (RF gain slider) - set_sql (squelch slider) - set_nb / set_nr / set_digisel / set_ipplus (toggle buttons)

    NOT receiver-specific (no change needed): - ptt — global - set_power — global TX power - select_vfo — VFO switch itself - vfo_swap / vfo_equalize — global

    2. Controls must show active receiver's values¶

    When switching VFO, all control elements must update to reflect the active receiver's state from the HTTP poll data.

    In applyStateDiff(): 

    var activeChanged = !prev || prev.active !== next.active;
if (activeChanged) {
    setActiveVfo(next.active === 'SUB' ? 'B' : 'A', false);

    // Update all controls from the new active receiver's state
    var rx = next.active === 'SUB' ? next.sub : next.main;
    updateModeButtons(rx.mode);
    updateFilterButtons(rx.filter);
    updateAttButtons(rx.att);
    updatePreButtons(rx.preamp);
    updateSliders(rx.af_level, rx.rf_gain, rx.squelch);
    updateToggleButtons(rx.nb, rx.nr, rx.digisel, rx.ipplus);
}

    3. Frequency display mapping¶

    VFO A panel always shows state.main.freq (MAIN receiver). VFO B panel always shows state.sub.freq (SUB receiver). The "active" VFO is visually highlighted (larger text, glow) — this already works.

    Tuning (wheel/click) must modify the active receiver's frequency: 

    function getActiveFreq() {
    return state.activeVfo === 'B' ? state.freqB : state.freqA;
}

function setActiveFreq(hz) {
    var rx = state.activeVfo === 'B' ? 1 : 0;
    sendCommand('set_freq', { freq: hz, receiver: rx });
}

    4. Waterfall click tuning¶

    Currently waterfall click calculates frequency from cursor position and sends set_freq. Must include receiver: 

    // In scopeClick handler:
var rx = state.activeVfo === 'B' ? 1 : 0;
sendCommand('set_freq', { freq: clickedFreq, receiver: rx });

    5. Mode-dependent tuning step¶

    When switching VFO, the tuning step should update based on the new active receiver's mode (e.g., CW=10Hz, USB=1kHz).

    Testing¶

    • Switch to VFO B → tune frequency → verify SUB freq changes on radio
    • Switch to VFO B → change mode → verify SUB mode changes
    • Switch to VFO B → adjust AF gain → verify SUB AF changes
    • Switch VFO back to A → verify controls revert to MAIN values
    • Physical VFO switch on radio → verify frontend follows (depends on #96)

    ✅ #95: Phase 3: SUB scope support (scope follows active receiver)¶

    Labels: enhancement, ui

    Parent: #92¶

    Depends on: #93 (cmd29 routing), #94 (receiver-aware frontend)¶

    Goal¶

    When the active receiver switches to SUB, the scope/waterfall must show SUB band data. Graceful fallback if Dual Watch is not enabled.

    Current State (partially implemented on branch feature/sub-scope-92)¶

    Commit cdd3c1d already implements: - SwitchScopeReceiver(receiver) command in radio_poller.py - scope_main_sub(receiver) CI-V command (0x27 0x12 0x00/0x01) sent after VFO switch - Frontend state.scope.activeReceiver tracking - renderLoop accepts frames matching active receiver (removed hardcoded receiver !== 0) - Waterfall clear on receiver switch - Zero-frame fallback (45 frames → switch back to MAIN + toast) - "MAIN"/"SUB" badge on spectrum panel - switch_scope_receiver command in ControlHandler - 7 new tests

    What still needs work¶

    1. Tie scope switching to the new receiver routing (from #93/#94)¶

    Currently SelectVfo in the poller calculates receiver from VFO name. After #93, it should use the same receiver value that's used for cmd29 routing: 

    case SelectVfo(vfo=vfo):
    ...
    # scope_main_sub should use the same receiver as everything else
    self._queue.put(SwitchScopeReceiver(receiver))

    2. Scope frame frequency axis¶

    When switching to SUB, the scope's frequency range changes completely (e.g., MAIN on 14 MHz, SUB on 7 MHz). The axis labels, passband overlay, and center marker must update from the SUB receiver's frequency, not MAIN's.

    The scope frame itself carries startFreq/endFreq — this should self-correct once we accept SUB frames. But the passband overlay uses state.mode and state.filter which must also come from the active receiver (handled in #94).

    3. Scope health monitor awareness¶

    server.py:_scope_health_monitor() re-enables scope after 10s of zero frames. When SUB scope is active and Dual Watch is off, this will fight with the frontend fallback.

    Fix: scope health monitor should know which receiver's scope is active and not re-enable when the zero-pixel condition is expected (SUB without DW).

    4. CI-V command sequence¶

    From testing documented in original issue #92: - Working sequence: scope_on → data_output_on → dual_scope_on → scope_main_sub(last) - Only scope_main_sub needs to be sent on VFO switch (scope already on + outputting) - 25ms delay between VFO switch and scope switch prevents CI-V flooding

    5. Dual Watch detection (from #96)¶

    Once #96 adds Dual Watch polling (0x07 0xC2), the scope UI can show a better indicator: - DW on + SUB scope: normal rendering - DW off + SUB scope: show "Enable Dual Watch on radio for SUB scope" instead of waiting 3s for fallback

    CI-V Commands¶

    Command Bytes Effect
    scope_main_sub(0) 0x27 0x12 0x00 Show MAIN scope
    scope_main_sub(1) 0x27 0x12 0x01 Show SUB scope
    scope_single_dual(true) 0x27 0x13 0x01 Enable dual scope mode

    Testing¶

    • Switch to SUB → scope shows SUB band → waterfall clears → axis updates
    • Switch back to MAIN → scope shows MAIN band
    • SUB scope without Dual Watch → fallback to MAIN after ~3s + toast
    • Rapidly switching MAIN↔SUB → no CI-V watchdog timeout or reconnect
    • Scope health monitor doesn't interfere with SUB scope fallback

    ✅ #96: Phase 4: Bidirectional sync & Dual Watch state polling¶

    Labels: enhancement

    Parent: #92¶

    Depends on: #93, #94¶

    Goal¶

    The web UI must stay in sync with the physical radio. If the user switches VFO or toggles Dual Watch on the radio's front panel, the web UI must follow. Currently the web UI only knows about changes it initiated.

    What to implement¶

    1. Poll 0x07 0xD2 — Active Receiver Query¶

    File: src/icom_lan/web/radio_poller.py

    Add to _STATE_QUERIES: 

    (0x07, 0xD2, None),   # Active receiver (MAIN=0x00, SUB=0x01) — global

    File: src/icom_lan/_civ_rx.py

    In _update_state_cache_from_frame(), parse the response: 

    if frame.command == 0x07 and frame.sub == 0xD2 and frame.data:
    new_active = "SUB" if frame.data[0] else "MAIN"
    rs = getattr(self, "_radio_state", None)
    if rs is not None and rs.active != new_active:
        rs.active = new_active
        if self._on_state_change:
            self._on_state_change("active_receiver_changed", {"active": new_active})

    This is exactly how wfview handles it (funcVFOBandMS in wfmain.cpp:5656-5676).

    2. Poll 0x07 0xC2 — Dual Watch Query¶

    File: src/icom_lan/web/radio_poller.py

    Add to _STATE_QUERIES: 

    (0x07, 0xC2, None),   # Dual Watch status — global

    File: src/icom_lan/radio_state.py

    Add field: 

    @dataclass(slots=True)
class RadioState:
    ...
    dual_watch: bool = False

    File: src/icom_lan/_civ_rx.py

    Parse response: 

    if frame.command == 0x07 and frame.sub == 0xC2 and frame.data:
    rs.dual_watch = bool(frame.data[0])

    3. Frontend reacts to receiver changes from radio¶

    File: src/icom_lan/web/static/index.html

    applyStateDiff() already reads active from HTTP poll. If the radio changes active (detected via 0x07 0xD2 polling), the next HTTP poll will return the new value, and the frontend will update — VFO highlight, control values, scope receiver.

    Add dual_watch to the state poll response and display indicator: 

    if (next.dual_watch !== undefined) {
    state.dualWatch = next.dual_watch;
    updateDualWatchIndicator();
}

    4. Dual Watch indicator in UI¶

    Show a small DW badge near the scope or VFO area: - DW ON: green badge "DW" — SUB scope available - DW OFF: dim/hidden — SUB scope will show zero pixels

    This replaces the 3-second zero-frame detection with immediate awareness.

    5. Remove 0x07 0xD0/0xD1 from SelectVfo?¶

    Question to decide: Should select_vfo still send 0x07 0xD0/0xD1 to switch the active receiver on the radio? Or should it only update the UI's active receiver and route cmd29 commands accordingly?

    Arguments for keeping 0x07 0xD0/0xD1: - The radio display follows the web UI (consistent visual) - Audio on LAN may follow the active receiver (needs testing — see #97)

    Arguments against: - With cmd29, we don't need to change the radio's active VFO to control a specific receiver - Changing active VFO has side effects on the radio's physical display

    Recommendation: Keep sending 0x07 0xD0/0xD1 for now. It's what the user expects — "I clicked SUB, the radio should show SUB". The cmd29 routing in #93 ensures commands go to the right receiver regardless.

    6. Handle VFO Swap sync¶

    When vfo_swap is used (0x07 0xB0), the radio swaps MAIN↔SUB frequencies. The poller currently fires a vfo_swapped event. After swap, active receiver stays the same but frequencies are exchanged. The HTTP poll will pick up the new frequencies automatically.

    Testing¶

    • Switch VFO physically on the radio → web UI updates within 1 poll cycle (~250ms)
    • Toggle Dual Watch on radio → DW indicator updates in web UI
    • Web UI switch + radio switch race condition → no desync
    • DW indicator shows correct state after server restart

    ✅ #97: Investigate: LAN audio receiver routing on IC-7610¶

    Labels: enhancement, area:audio

    Parent: #92¶

    Question¶

    Does switching the active receiver via 0x07 0xD1 (SUB Select) change which receiver's audio is sent over the LAN UDP audio port?

    Context¶

    IC-7610 LAN protocol sends a single audio stream on UDP port 50003. Both receivers are always active simultaneously. The question is: whose audio do we get?

    Possibilities¶

    1. Audio follows active receiver — after 0x07 0xD1, LAN audio switches to SUB. This is the simple case and would mean our current approach works.

    2. Audio is always MAIN — LAN audio is hardwired to MAIN receiver output regardless of VFO selection. Would need a different approach.

    3. Audio is mixed — both receivers' audio is mixed into the stream. Switching VFO just changes what the user hears on the speaker but the LAN stream is the same.

    How to test¶

    1. Tune MAIN to a frequency with strong signal (e.g., FT8 on 14.074)
    2. Tune SUB to a quiet frequency (or vice versa)
    3. Switch to SUB via web UI
    4. Listen to LAN audio — does it change?
    5. Check with radio speaker vs LAN to compare

    wfview reference¶

    wfview has a single audio handler per receiver widget (receiverwidget.cpp). On IC-7610 with 2 receivers, it creates 2 receiver widgets but likely routes the single LAN audio stream. Need to investigate how wfview handles this.

    If audio does NOT follow VFO switch¶

    May need to investigate: - Whether there's a separate CI-V command to select audio routing - Whether the radio needs to be in a specific mode (e.g., split) for audio to follow - Whether this is a fundamental LAN protocol limitation

    Priority¶

    Lower than phases 1-4. Audio works for MAIN receiver. This is about making it work for SUB.

    ✅ #98: Add SECURITY.md — document protocol risks and VPN recommendation¶

    Labels: documentation, priority:P1

    Problem¶

    The Icom LAN protocol uses a substitution cipher for login credentials — this is obfuscation, not encryption. All traffic (including audio, CI-V commands, PTT) travels over UDP in effectively cleartext.

    Anyone on the same network (or on the internet if ports are forwarded) can: - Intercept credentials - Take full control of the transceiver - Key PTT (transmit) without authorization

    This is a protocol limitation — we cannot fix it in code. But we must document the risk clearly.

    What to create¶

    1. SECURITY.md in project root¶

    Contents: - Protocol Security Warning — explain that Icom's LAN protocol provides only obfuscation, not real encryption - Threat Model — sniffing, replay, unauthorized PTT - Recommendations: - Never expose UDP ports 50001-50003 directly to the internet - Use VPN for remote access (WireGuard, Tailscale, ZeroTier) - Restrict LAN access to trusted devices - Consider firewall rules limiting access to radio's IP - Disclosure — this is not a vulnerability in icom-lan; it's a limitation of the Icom protocol shared by all implementations (wfview, win4icom, etc.)

    2. README.md security section¶

    Add a prominent ⚠️ Security section near the top of README linking to SECURITY.md: 

    ## ⚠️ Security Notice

The Icom LAN protocol provides **no encryption**. Credentials and all radio control 
traffic can be intercepted on the network. **Never expose radio ports to the internet** 
without a VPN. See [SECURITY.md](SECURITY.md) for details.

    3. CLI startup warning (optional)¶

    Consider printing a one-time warning when connecting over a non-private IP range: 

    WARNING: Icom LAN protocol is unencrypted. Use VPN for remote access.

    Priority¶

    High — this is a responsibility issue. Users connecting their transceivers remotely need to understand the risk.

    ✅ #99: Audit and fix broad exception swallowing (except Exception: pass)¶

    Labels: bug, priority:P2

    Problem¶

    The codebase has numerous instances of except Exception: pass in "best-effort" code paths. This anti-pattern masks real bugs (KeyError, AttributeError, TypeError) by silently swallowing them, making them appear as transient network issues.

    Examples of problematic patterns¶

    # Scope callback — hides bugs in callback logic
try:
    callback(frame)
except Exception:
    logger.exception("Scope callback raised an exception")  # at least this one logs

# State cache updates — hides parsing bugs
try:
    if cache.freq_ts > 0.0:
        data["freq_a"] = cache.freq
except Exception as exc:
    logger.debug("control: state cache read failed: %s", exc)

# Scope health check — completely silent
try:
    pixels = getattr(frame, "pixels", None) or b""
    if any(b != 0 for b in pixels):
        self._scope_last_nonzero = time.monotonic()
except Exception:
    pass  # ← bug could hide here forever

    What to do¶

    1. Audit all except Exception in the codebase¶

    grep -rn "except Exception" src/icom_lan/ --include="*.py"

    2. For each instance, classify:¶

    Replace with specific exceptions: - Network/transport errors → except (TimeoutError, ConnectionError, OSError) - CI-V parse errors → except (ValueError, IndexError) - Queue operations → except (asyncio.QueueFull, asyncio.QueueEmpty)

    Add logging for unexpected errors: 

    # Before (bad):
except Exception:
    pass

# After (good):
except (TimeoutError, ConnectionError, OSError):
    pass  # expected transient network error
except Exception:
    logger.warning("unexpected error in scope_health_check", exc_info=True)

    3. Key files to audit¶

    File Likely count Risk
    src/icom_lan/radio.py High Hides CI-V parsing bugs
    src/icom_lan/_civ_rx.py Medium Hides frame routing bugs
    src/icom_lan/web/server.py Medium Hides scope/meter broadcast bugs
    src/icom_lan/web/handlers.py Low Audio callback errors
    src/icom_lan/_control_phase.py Medium Hides auth/handshake bugs
    src/icom_lan/_audio_recovery.py Medium Hides codec/jitter bugs

    4. Testing¶

    After fixes, run full test suite to verify no regressions. Some tests may have been passing because exceptions were silently swallowed — those are real bugs that should be fixed.

    Priority¶

    Medium — not user-facing, but significantly impacts debuggability. Every silent pass is a potential hours-long debugging session.

    ✅ #100: Abstract Radio interface — multi-radio foundation¶

    Labels: enhancement, priority:P0

    Summary¶

    Extract an abstract Radio protocol/interface from IcomRadio to support multiple radio backends with a single Web UI.

    Motivation¶

    Currently icom-lan only supports Icom radios via LAN protocol. To support Yaesu, Xiegu, Lab599, and serial-connected Icom radios, we need a common interface that all backends implement.

    Design¶

    ┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐
│            Web UI (единый)              │
├─────────────────────────────────────────┤
│         Abstract Radio Interface        │
├──────┬──────┬──────┬──────┬────────────┤
│ Icom │ Icom │Yaesu │Xiegu │ Lab599     │
│ LAN  │Serial│ CAT  │ CAT  │ CAT        │
└──────┴──────┴──────┴──────┴────────────┘

    Tasks¶

    • [ ] Define Radio Protocol (Python protocol class) with core methods: get_frequency, set_frequency, get_mode, set_mode, set_ptt, get_s_meter, audio RX/TX, scope data
    • [ ] Refactor IcomRadio to implement the protocol
    • [ ] Refactor WebServer/RadioPoller to depend on abstract Radio, not IcomRadio
    • [ ] Factory function: create_radio(type, config) -> Radio
    • [ ] Update CLI to accept --radio-type parameter

    Notes¶

    This is the foundation for all other multi-radio features. Should be done before adding new backends.

    ⬜ #101: IC-7300 enablement on serial backend (downstream of #152)¶

    Labels: enhancement, priority:P3

    Summary¶

    Enable IC-7300 on top of the new shared-core serial architecture.

    This issue is now a downstream model-enablement task and depends on the backend foundation tracked in #152.

    Positioning¶

    • This is not the place to design serial architecture from scratch anymore.
    • Serial architecture and shared IC-7610 core are tracked in: #152 (with sub-issues #141-#151).
    • This issue starts after the required foundation is merged.

    Depends on¶

    • [ ] #152 epic(backends): IC-7610 shared core + USB serial/audio backend

    Goal (post-#152)¶

    Implement IC-7300-specific profile/routing/capability support using the existing serial backend infrastructure.

    Agent Context¶

    Relevant code after #152 baseline: - backend factory/config + contracts - shared core + serial link/session - capability/profile layer from #119 direction

    Likely touch points: - src/icom_lan/radios.py (or new profile registry module) - backend model/profile mapping - command routing guards for model-specific feature support - CLI/Web/rigctld capability behavior validation

    Scope¶

    • Add IC-7300 profile to backend model registry.
    • Validate/adjust CI-V command support matrix for IC-7300.
    • Ensure unsupported features fail clearly (scope/dual_rx/etc as applicable).
    • Validate end-to-end user flows through API + CLI + rigctld + Web using capability gates.

    Explicitly out of scope¶

    • Re-architecting serial backend internals.
    • Reworking shared core extraction.

    Implementation Checklist¶

    • [ ] Add/verify IC-7300 profile (civ addr, capabilities, command29 policy, feature flags).
    • [ ] Add model-specific command guardrails (unsupported commands -> explicit CommandError).
    • [ ] Validate mode/frequency/PTT/power/meter command behavior on IC-7300 profile.
    • [ ] Ensure CLI/Web/rigctld surfaces reflect IC-7300 capabilities correctly.
    • [ ] Add tests for IC-7300 profile path (unit + contract + integration-marked where possible).

    Acceptance Criteria¶

    • [ ] IC-7300 runs through serial backend without architecture-level hacks.
    • [ ] Capability reporting is accurate and consumed correctly by Web/rigctld/CLI.
    • [ ] Unsupported feature calls fail predictably with actionable messages.
    • [ ] Regression: LAN + IC-7610 serial tests remain green.

    Hardware Notes¶

    • IC-7300 available for testing.
    • USB serial paths: /dev/cu.usbserial-* (macOS), /dev/ttyUSB* (Linux).

    ✅ #102: Mobile-friendly responsive UI (PWA)¶

    Labels: enhancement, ui, priority:P3

    Summary¶

    Make the web UI fully usable on mobile phones (portrait, <768px) with touch-friendly controls, grouped dropdowns, swipe-to-tune, and hold-to-talk PTT.

    Design¶

    Design doc: docs/plans/2026-03-04-mobile-ui-design.md Implementation plan: docs/plans/2026-03-04-mobile-ui-plan.md

    Mobile Layout (Portrait)¶

    ┌─────────────────────────┐
│ HEADER: IC-7610 · 🟢     │
├─────────────────────────┤
│ VFO A        14.074.000 │  swipe = tune
│ USB · FIL1    [MAIN|sub]│
├─────────────────────────┤
│ ┌─────────────────────┐ │
│ │ SPECTRUM + WATERFALL │ │  ~30%, tap→fullscreen
│ └─────────────────────┘ │
├─────────────────────────┤
│[BAND▾][MODE▾][FIL▾][FEAT▾]│
│ S ████████░░░  S7       │
│ SWR 1.0  PWR 50W       │
├─────────────────────────┤
│ 🔊 Audio │  [===PTT===] │  fixed bottom bar
└─────────────────────────┘

    Implementation Phases¶

    Phase 1: Base Responsive¶

    • [x] Design doc
    • [x] Implementation plan
    • [ ] Viewport meta + CSS grid restructure
    • [ ] Hide desktop-only elements on mobile

    Phase 2: VFO + Controls¶

    • [ ] Single VFO display + MAIN/SUB toggle
    • [ ] Large frequency (2.5rem), hide badges
    • [ ] Grouped dropdowns: BAND, MODE, FILTER, FEATURES
    • [ ] Custom overlay (not <select>), dark theme

    Phase 3: Waterfall¶

    • [ ] Compact waterfall (~35vh)
    • [ ] Fullscreen overlay (tap to enter, ✕ to exit)
    • [ ] Auto-hide header (3s), tap-to-tune in fullscreen

    Phase 4: Touch Gestures¶

    • [ ] Swipe-to-tune on frequency display
    • [ ] Velocity-dependent step (slow=100Hz, fast=1-5kHz)
    • [ ] Mode-aware default step (CW:100, SSB:1k, FM:5k)

    Phase 5: Audio + PTT¶

    • [ ] iOS AudioContext fix (user gesture required)
    • [ ] Audio toggle button (bottom bar)
    • [ ] PTT hold-to-talk (touchstart/end, 200ms anti-bounce)
    • [ ] getUserMedia for phone mic TX

    Phase 6: Meters + Polish¶

    • [ ] Compact inline meters (S-meter bar + SWR/PWR/Vd)
    • [ ] iOS safe area insets
    • [ ] Prevent zoom, horizontal scroll
    • [ ] Transitions, edge cases

    Phase 7 (deferred): PWA¶

    • [ ] Manifest + service worker
    • [ ] Install to home screen
    • [ ] Offline shell

    Technical Notes¶

    • Single file: src/icom_lan/web/static/index.html (2695 lines)
    • All mobile changes gated behind @media (max-width: 767px) or .mobile-only class
    • Desktop layout (≥768px) completely unchanged
    • Tailwind CSS + vanilla JS (no framework)

    ✅ #103: SDRplay RSPdx wideband panadapter¶

    Labels: enhancement

    Summary¶

    Use SDRplay RSPdx as a wideband panadapter alongside the main transceiver. Second waterfall showing entire band (e.g. full 40m), click-to-tune the transceiver.

    Motivation¶

    IC-7610 scope shows ±25/50/100 kHz. RSPdx can show up to 10 MHz bandwidth — the entire band at once. Clicking a signal on the wide waterfall tunes the transceiver.

    Design¶

    ┌──────────────────────────────────────┐
│ SDRplay: 7.000 - 7.300 MHz (full 40m)│ ← wideband
├──────────────────────────────────────┤
│ IC-7610: 7.140 ± 50 kHz              │ ← narrowband
├──────────────────────────────────────┤
│ Audio   [VFO A: 7.142.000 LSB]       │
└──────────────────────────────────────┘

    Tasks¶

    • [ ] SoapySDR Python bindings for RSPdx
    • [ ] FFT pipeline: IQ samples → spectrum data → WebSocket
    • [ ] Second waterfall canvas in web UI (above or below radio scope)
    • [ ] Click-to-tune: click on wide waterfall → set transceiver frequency
    • [ ] Band tracking: wide waterfall follows transceiver band changes
    • [ ] Configurable: center freq, bandwidth, FFT size, averaging
    • [ ] Optional: signal markers (overlay DX spots, band plan)

    Hardware¶

    • SDRplay RSPdx available
    • SoapySDR + SoapySDRPlay driver
    • Antenna: can share via splitter or use separate RX antenna

    Dependencies¶

    • numpy (FFT)
    • soapysdr (SDR access)
    • Existing waterfall renderer (Canvas API) can be reused

    ⬜ #104: WebRTC audio for lower latency¶

    Labels: enhancement, area:audio, priority:P3

    Summary¶

    Replace custom WebSocket audio transport with WebRTC for significantly lower latency and better browser audio handling.

    Motivation¶

    Current path: Icom UDP Opus → server decode → PCM → WebSocket → browser decode → AudioWorklet. Multiple encode/decode cycles add latency.

    WebRTC provides: P2P audio with hardware echo cancellation, adaptive jitter buffer, automatic bitrate adjustment, and native browser support. Expected 3-5x latency reduction.

    Current Architecture¶

    Radio → UDP Opus → Server → PCM → WS binary → Browser AudioWorklet
Browser Mic → WS binary → Server → Opus encode → UDP → Radio

    Proposed Architecture¶

    Radio → UDP Opus → Server (aiortc) ← WebRTC → Browser
Browser Mic → WebRTC → Server (aiortc) → Opus → UDP → Radio

    Tasks¶

    • [ ] Add aiortc dependency (Python WebRTC)
    • [ ] WebRTC signaling endpoint (/api/v1/rtc/offer, /api/v1/rtc/answer)
    • [ ] Server-side: bridge Icom audio ↔ WebRTC audio track
    • [ ] Client-side: RTCPeerConnection replacing AudioWorklet WS consumer
    • [ ] Fallback: keep WS audio for browsers without WebRTC
    • [ ] TX audio: microphone → WebRTC → server → Icom TX
    • [ ] Measure and compare latency (before/after)

    Notes¶

    • aiortc is pure Python WebRTC — no native dependencies
    • ICE/STUN not needed for same-network (direct connection)
    • For remote access via VPN: TURN server may be needed

    ✅ #105: Built-in FT8/FT4 decoder and auto-QSO¶

    Labels: enhancement

    Summary¶

    Integrate FT8/FT4 decoding directly into the web UI — decode signals from the radio audio stream, display decoded messages, and enable one-click QSO.

    Motivation¶

    Current workflow: WSJT-X → flrig → rigctld → radio. Four separate programs with complex configuration. If the decoder is built into our web UI, it's zero-config and works remotely from any browser.

    Design¶

    Audio RX → FFT → FT8 decode → Message table → Click → Auto TX sequence
                                    ↓
                              Waterfall overlay (signal markers)

    Features¶

    • [ ] FT8 decoder (Python port or ft8_lib / wsjtx-utils bindings)
    • [ ] FT4 decoder
    • [ ] Decoded messages table in UI (callsign, grid, SNR, freq offset)
    • [ ] Waterfall overlay: decoded signal markers
    • [ ] Click-to-call: select a station → auto-fill TX message → QSO sequence
    • [ ] TX message generation (CQ, report, RR73)
    • [ ] Audio TX injection: generate FT8 tones → radio TX audio
    • [ ] Auto-sequencing: full QSO without manual intervention
    • [ ] Frequency calibration: sync with radio's exact frequency
    • [ ] Integration with logging (#TBD)

    Technical Notes¶

    • FT8 decode needs 12.6s audio windows at 48kHz
    • Decode is CPU-bound — consider numpy/numba optimization
    • Existing audio RX stream provides the input
    • TX audio generation: numpy sine wave synthesis → push to radio
    • Reference: WSJT-X source (Fortran), ft8_lib (C), jtdx

    Ambitious but achievable¶

    • Core FT8 decode: ~500 lines Python with numpy
    • TX tone generation: ~100 lines
    • UI table + waterfall overlay: ~200 lines JS
    • Auto-sequencing: ~300 lines Python state machine

    ✅ #106: Virtual audio devices — system mic/speaker for WSJT-X, fldigi, etc.¶

    Labels: enhancement, area:audio, priority:P1

    Summary¶

    Expose IC-7610 LAN audio as standard system audio devices (virtual microphone + virtual speaker). Any software (WSJT-X, fldigi, JS8Call, etc.) can use the radio as a normal sound card — no special configuration.

    Motivation¶

    Currently, digital mode software (WSJT-X, JTDX, JS8Call, fldigi) requires either: - Physical USB cable to the radio, OR - flrig/rigctld + virtual audio cables (VB-Cable, Soundflower, PulseAudio loopback)

    This is fragile, OS-specific, and breaks remote operation. If icom-lan creates virtual audio devices that bridge LAN audio ↔ system audio, any software works out of the box — locally or remotely.

    Design¶

    ┌─────────────┐     ┌──────────────┐     ┌─────────────┐
│  IC-7610    │ UDP │  icom-lan    │ sys │  WSJT-X     │
│  LAN audio  │◄───►│  server      │◄───►│  fldigi     │
│  (Opus/PCM) │     │              │     │  JS8Call     │
└─────────────┘     │  Virtual Mic │     │  Any app    │
                    │  Virtual Spk │     └─────────────┘
                    └──────────────┘
    • Virtual Microphone (output device from our perspective): radio RX audio → system audio input. WSJT-X sees it as "icom-lan RX"
    • Virtual Speaker (input device from our perspective): system audio output → radio TX audio. WSJT-X sends to "icom-lan TX"

    Platform Support¶

    Platform Virtual Audio Backend
    macOS CoreAudio HAL plugin, or BlackHole/Soundflower integration
    Linux PulseAudio null-sink / PipeWire virtual device
    Windows Virtual Audio Cable API / WASAPI loopback

    Simplest cross-platform: use sounddevice (PortAudio) to write RX to a loopback device and read TX from another.

    Tasks¶

    • [ ] RX path: radio Opus/PCM → decode → write to virtual mic device
    • [ ] TX path: read from virtual speaker device → encode → radio TX audio
    • [ ] Auto-create virtual devices on startup (platform-specific)
    • [ ] CLI: icom-lan audio-bridge mode (standalone, no web UI required)
    • [ ] Also integrate into web mode (run alongside web UI)
    • [ ] Sample rate matching: radio 48kHz ↔ device sample rate
    • [ ] Latency monitoring and buffer tuning
    • [ ] Device naming: "icom-lan RX (IC-7610)" / "icom-lan TX (IC-7610)"
    • [ ] Multiple radios: one pair of virtual devices per radio instance

    Use Cases¶

    1. WSJT-X remote: radio in shack, WSJT-X on laptop, connected via VPN
    2. fldigi/JS8Call: same setup, any digital mode software
    3. Recording: pipe RX audio to Audacity or OBS
    4. Streaming: pipe RX to Discord/Mumble for net operations
    5. Contest logging: N1MM/Log4OM with audio recording

    Dependencies¶

    • sounddevice (PortAudio bindings) — cross-platform audio I/O
    • Platform-specific: BlackHole (macOS), PipeWire (Linux), VB-Cable (Windows)
    • Existing audio RX/TX code in icom-lan already handles Opus encode/decode

    ⬜ #107: Yaesu CAT backend (FTX-1, FT-710, FT-991A, etc.)¶

    Labels: enhancement, priority:P3

    Summary¶

    Add Yaesu CAT protocol backend to support Yaesu transceivers via serial/USB.

    Motivation¶

    Yaesu CAT is one of the most common radio control protocols. Supporting it opens icom-lan to a huge range of radios: FTX-1, FT-710, FT-991A, FT-DX10, FT-DX101, FT-891, FT-818, etc.

    Depends On¶

    • 100 (Abstract Radio interface) — must be implemented first¶

    Yaesu CAT Protocol¶

    • Serial (USB): 4800–38400 baud, 8N2 (most models)
    • Command format: ASCII-based, semicolon-terminated (e.g. FA007074000; = set VFO-A freq)
    • Response format: same ASCII with trailing semicolon
    • Key commands: FA/FB (freq), MD (mode), TX/RX (PTT), SM (S-meter), RM (SWR), AG (AF gain)
    • No audio over CAT — use USB audio device or virtual audio (#106)
    • No scope data over CAT — would need SDR or dedicated scope feed

    Tasks¶

    • [ ] Depends on #100
    • [ ] YaesuCatTransport — serial async transport
    • [ ] YaesuRadio implementing abstract Radio protocol
    • [ ] CAT command builder/parser
    • [ ] Radio model profiles (FTX-1, FT-710, etc. — command variations)
    • [ ] Polling loop for S-meter, frequency tracking
    • [ ] CLI: icom-lan yaesu --port /dev/cu.usbserial-XXX --model ftx1 web

    Hardware Available¶

    • Yaesu FTX-1 (portable QRP SDR) for testing

    References¶

    • Yaesu CAT Command Reference (per model)
    • hamlib Yaesu backend source code
    • flrig Yaesu implementation

    ✅ #108: DX cluster spots on waterfall¶

    Labels: enhancement, ui, priority:P3

    Summary¶

    Display real-time DX cluster spots as markers/labels directly on the waterfall display. Click a spot to tune the transceiver.

    Motivation¶

    DX cluster is the primary way hams discover active stations. Currently requires a separate app (DXSpider telnet client, web cluster). Integrating spots into the waterfall makes them instantly actionable — see a callsign on the waterfall, click, work.

    Design¶

    Waterfall:
  ▼ JA1ABC (Japan)        ▼ DL5XX (Germany)
  ════════════════════════════════════════
  ░░░░░█░░░░░░░░░░░░░░█░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░
  ░░░░░█░░░░░░░░░░░░░░█░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░
  7.000          7.050          7.100

    Features¶

    • [ ] Telnet DX cluster client (DXSpider, AR-Cluster, CC Cluster)
    • [ ] WebSocket-based cluster aggregator (optional: web cluster APIs)
    • [ ] Spot markers on waterfall canvas (callsign, frequency, time)
    • [ ] Color coding: new spots (bright), aging spots (dim), worked (green)
    • [ ] Click-to-tune: click spot → set transceiver frequency + mode
    • [ ] Spot filtering: by band, mode, continent, DXCC entity
    • [ ] Sidebar: scrolling spot list with callsign, freq, spotter, time
    • [ ] Self-spot suppression (filter own callsign)
    • [ ] Integration with QRZ.com API (flag, name, DXCC on hover)

    Tasks¶

    • [ ] Telnet client: connect to cluster node (e.g. telnet dxc.nc7j.com 7373)
    • [ ] Spot parser: DX de callsign format → structured data
    • [ ] WebSocket broadcast: spots → all connected web clients
    • [ ] Canvas overlay: draw spot markers on waterfall
    • [ ] Click handler: spot click → set_frequency + set_mode
    • [ ] Filter UI: band/mode/continent dropdowns
    • [ ] Persistence: remember cluster server, filters, own callsign

    Configuration¶

    dx_cluster:
  server: dxc.nc7j.com
  port: 7373
  callsign: KN4KYD
  filters:
    bands: [40m, 20m, 15m, 10m]
    modes: [CW, SSB, FT8]

    Dependencies¶

    • Telnet client (asyncio, stdlib)
    • No external dependencies needed
    • QRZ.com API (optional, needs API key)

    ⬜ #109: Audio bridge: custom device naming (icom-lan RX/TX)¶

    Labels: enhancement, area:audio, priority:P3

    Name virtual audio devices descriptively: "icom-lan RX (IC-7610)" / "icom-lan TX (IC-7610)" instead of generic BlackHole names. Requires CoreAudio HAL plugin or aggregate device with alias.

    Split from #106 MVP.

    ✅ #110: Audio bridge: latency monitoring and stats¶

    Labels: enhancement, area:audio, priority:P2
